message:
feat(btree,vdbe): push collation-aware key compare down into BtCursor + thread registry from VDBE

Complete the collation-aware UNIQUE/index work started in the
earlier commits in this batch by pushing the collation logic
from the VDBE helpers (storage_cursor_no_conflict_prefix_match_collated,
storage_cursor_exact_match_collated, find_conflicting_rowid_in_index_collated)
down into BtCursor itself, so every BTree key comparison during
seek/next/prev consults the correct collation.

Before this commit, the VDBE wrapper functions would iterate
the cursor and compare decoded values with collation, but the
cursor's own seek/next/prev logic still used bytewise
PartialOrd comparisons.  That was fine for exact-match probes
(which re-compared every row manually) but broke range queries
and ORDER BY on NOCASE/RTRIM indexes — a BETWEEN or > on a
collated index would stop at the wrong row.

crates/fsqlite-btree/Cargo.toml
Cargo.lock
- Add fsqlite-func = { workspace = true } to fsqlite-btree's
  [dependencies] so BtCursor can import CollationRegistry
  directly instead of routing through a trait at the cursor
  layer.  The Cargo.lock update just records the new
  dependency edge.

crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cursor.rs

- New imports:
    fsqlite_func::collation::CollationRegistry
    fsqlite_types::SqliteValue  (was already used via the
        fully-qualified path; now direct)
    std::sync::{Arc, Mutex}

- BtCursor gains two new fields:
    index_collations: Vec<Option<String>>
        Per-key collation name list.  None/missing entries
        imply BINARY.  The trailing rowid suffix has no entry
        since it's always compared as an integer.
    collation_registry: Arc<Mutex<CollationRegistry>>
        Shared registry for resolving named collations.
- Default constructor initializes both fields to empty/default
  so existing code paths that construct a BtCursor without
  collation context keep working with the bytewise fast path.

- New method set_index_collation_context(collations, registry):
  called by the VDBE right after cursor construction to hand
  over the collation metadata.  Stores the vec and the Arc
  directly.

- compare_index_keys() (the core cursor key comparator,
  previously `lhs[idx].partial_cmp(&rhs[idx])`) rewritten:
    1. Lock the collation_registry once at the top of the
       function so the per-position comparisons inside the
       loop don't re-lock.
    2. For each (lhs[idx], rhs[idx]) pair, look up the
       collation name via self.index_collations.get(idx) and
       delegate to the new cmp_index_values_collated helper.
    3. Apply the existing desc_flags handling afterwards.
    4. Fall back to the integer rowid comparison on the
       trailing suffix.

- New helper cmp_index_values_collated(lhs, rhs, coll_name,
  registry):
    - When both sides are SqliteValue::Text AND coll_name is
      Some, delegate to compare_text_with_collation.
    - Otherwise fall back to lhs.partial_cmp(rhs), which
      preserves the existing behavior for non-text values and
      BINARY columns.

- New helper compare_text_with_collation(left, right, coll_name,
  registry):
    - registry.find(coll_name) returns Some(&Collation) for
      BINARY/NOCASE/RTRIM/user-defined collations.
    - On a match, call collation.compare(left, right) which
      returns the canonical Ordering.
    - On a miss (unknown collation name), fall back to
      left.cmp(right) — defensive default matching SQLite's
      "unknown collation → BINARY" rule.

crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs

- Three cursor construction sites (the Phase-4 storage cursor
  opener, OpenRead, and OpenWrite) now call
  new_cursor.set_index_collation_context(
      self.index_collations_for_root(root_page),
      Arc::clone(&self.collation_registry),
  )
  immediately after BtCursor::new but only for index cursors
  (gated on `!is_table_btree`).  Table cursors don't have
  collations on their primary key, so skipping the call saves
  an Arc clone and a pair of writes per cursor.
- The Arc::clone keeps the registry shared across all active
  cursors and the rest of the VDBE, so collation registrations
  (CREATE COLLATION, user-supplied collations) propagate
  through immediately.

After this commit, a range query like
    SELECT * FROM t WHERE name BETWEEN 'foo' AND 'FOO'
against a table with a NOCASE index on `name` correctly
iterates the full NOCASE range at the btree level, without
the VDBE having to decode every row and re-compare.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
